questions.title: Masked Language Modeling on Protein Sequences
questions.description: Implement the masked language modeling (MLM) objective for a protein sequence. Randomly mask 15% of amino acid tokens and compute cross-entropy loss on the masked positions only.
questions.info: Masked language modeling (MLM) is the core pre-training objective used by ESM-family models. In MLM, a fraction of input tokens are replaced with a <mask> token, and the model is trained to predict the original identity from context. Rives et al. (PNAS 2021) demonstrated that applying BERT-style MLM to 250 million protein sequences produces representations that capture biological structure and function without any supervision. The masking rate of 15% follows the original BERT protocol. ESM-2 (Lin et al., Science 2023) scaled this approach to 15 billion parameters and showed that learned representations implicitly encode 3D protein structure. Reference: ESM-1b (Rives et al., PNAS 2021); ESM-2 (Lin et al., Science 2023).
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

def mask_sequence(token_ids, vocab_size, mask_token_id, mask_prob=0.15):
    """Randomly mask 15% of tokens in a protein sequence."""
    labels = token_ids.clone()
    # Create random mask with given probability
    probability_matrix = torch.full(labels.shape, mask_prob)
    masked_indices = {{b1}}
    # Set labels to -100 for non-masked positions (ignored in loss)
    labels[~masked_indices] = {{b2}}
    # Replace masked positions with mask_token_id
    token_ids[masked_indices] = {{b3}}
    return token_ids, labels

# Example usage
vocab_size = 33  # ESM-2 vocabulary size
seq = torch.randint(4, 24, (1, 50))  # random protein sequence
masked_seq, labels = mask_sequence(seq, vocab_size, mask_token_id=32)
print("Masked positions:", (labels != -100).sum().item())

questions.title: Extracting ESM-2 Embeddings
questions.description: Load the ESM-2 model from Hugging Face and extract per-residue embeddings for a protein sequence.
questions.info: ESM-2 (Lin et al., Science 2023) is the largest protein language model trained to date, scaling up to 15 billion parameters. It was trained on UniRef sequences using masked language modeling and produces per-residue embeddings that implicitly encode protein 3D structure. The authors demonstrated that a simple linear projection of ESM-2 embeddings can predict protein structures competitive with AlphaFold2 for single sequences. ESM-2 is available through Hugging Face Transformers via the EsmModel and EsmTokenizer classes. The model outputs last_hidden_state of shape (batch, seq_len, hidden_dim). Reference: ESM-2 (Lin et al., Science 2023).

questions.title: Contact Prediction from Attention Maps
questions.description: Extract attention maps from ESM-1b and use symmetrized attention for contact prediction. Contacts are residue pairs within 8 Angstroms in 3D space.
questions.info: Rao et al. (NeurIPS 2020, 'Transformer protein language models are unsupervised structure learners') showed that attention heads in protein language models learn to attend to residues that are in 3D contact. By extracting and symmetrizing the attention matrices, one can predict contacts without any structural supervision. ESM-1b (Rives et al., PNAS 2021) further improved on these results. The key insight is that certain attention heads specialize in capturing structural contacts. The symmetrization operation averages A and A^T to produce an undirected contact map. APC (Average Product Correction) is typically applied to remove phylogenetic biases. Reference: Rao et al. (NeurIPS 2020); ESM-1b (Rives et al., PNAS 2021).
questions.code:
import torch

def symmetrize_attention(attn):
    """Symmetrize attention matrix for contact prediction.
    attn: (num_heads, seq_len, seq_len)
    """
    # Average over all attention heads
    attn_mean = {{b1}}
    # Symmetrize: average A and A^T
    contact_map = {{b2}}
    return contact_map

def apc_correction(contact_map):
    """Apply Average Product Correction."""
    row_mean = contact_map.mean(dim=-1, keepdim=True)
    col_mean = contact_map.mean(dim=-2, keepdim=True)
    global_mean = {{b3}}
    corrected = contact_map - (row_mean * col_mean) / global_mean
    return corrected

# Simulated attention from 20 heads, sequence length 50
attn = torch.rand(20, 50, 50)
contacts = symmetrize_attention(attn)
corrected = apc_correction(contacts)
print("Contact map shape:", corrected.shape)

Average the matrix with its transpose to make it symmetric.
The global mean is the mean over the entire contact map.

questions.title: Amino Acid Tokenization
questions.description: Build a simple amino acid tokenizer for protein sequences using the standard 20 amino acids plus special tokens (<cls>, <pad>, <eos>, <unk>, <mask>).
questions.info: Protein language models tokenize sequences at the individual amino acid level, unlike NLP models that use subword tokenization like BPE. The standard vocabulary consists of 20 canonical amino acids (A, R, N, D, C, E, Q, G, H, I, L, K, M, F, P, S, T, W, Y, V) plus special tokens. ESM-2 (Lin et al., Science 2023) uses a vocabulary of 33 tokens including special tokens and ambiguous amino acids (B, J, O, U, X, Z). ProtTrans (Elnaggar et al., IEEE TPAMI 2022) similarly uses character-level tokenization but adds spaces between residues to leverage existing NLP tokenizers. The <cls> token embedding is often used as a whole-sequence representation. Reference: ESM-2 (Lin et al., Science 2023); ProtTrans (Elnaggar et al., IEEE TPAMI 2022).

blanks.hint: Unknown amino acids should map to the <unk> token.
Append the end-of-sequence token after the amino acids.
The vocabulary size is the length of the vocab dictionary.

questions.title: Fine-Tuning PLM for Sequence Classification
questions.description: Fine-tune an ESM-2 model for binary protein classification (e.g., enzyme vs non-enzyme) by adding a classification head on top of the <cls> token representation.
questions.info: Fine-tuning pre-trained protein language models for downstream tasks is a dominant paradigm in computational biology. Rives et al. (PNAS 2021) showed that ESM-1b representations transfer well to tasks including secondary structure prediction, contact prediction, and remote homology detection. The standard approach adds a task-specific head on top of the frozen or trainable PLM backbone. For sequence-level tasks, the <cls> token representation (index 0) is typically used as input to the classification head. Lin et al. (Science 2023) demonstrated that even simple linear probes on ESM-2 embeddings achieve strong performance. ProtTrans (Elnaggar et al., IEEE TPAMI 2022) showed similar transfer learning gains with T5-based protein models. Reference: ESM-1b (Rives et al., PNAS 2021); ESM-2 (Lin et al., Science 2023); ProtTrans (Elnaggar et al., IEEE TPAMI 2022).

import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class ProteinClassifier(nn.Module):

questions.title: Zero-Shot Fitness Prediction with Masked Marginals
questions.description: Use ESM-2 to score mutations by computing the log-likelihood ratio between mutant and wildtype amino acids at a masked position (masked marginal approach).
questions.info: Meier et al. (NeurIPS 2021, 'Language models enable zero-shot prediction of the effects of mutations on protein fitness') demonstrated that protein LMs can predict mutational fitness without any task-specific training. The masked marginal scoring approach masks each position and computes the log-probability ratio log P(mutant) - log P(wildtype) from the model's softmax output. This method outperformed EVmutation and DeepSequence on multiple deep mutational scanning datasets. ESM-1v, a variant specifically trained for variant effect prediction, was shown to capture epistatic effects. The key advantage is that no multiple sequence alignment (MSA) is needed, unlike evolutionary methods. Reference: Meier et al. (NeurIPS 2021); ESM-1v (Meier et al., NeurIPS 2021).

questions.title: ESM-2 Transformer Architecture
questions.description: Define the core components of the ESM-2 transformer encoder architecture for protein sequences, including rotary position embeddings.
questions.info: ESM-2 (Lin et al., Science 2023) uses a standard transformer encoder architecture with several modifications. Unlike ESM-1b which used learned absolute position embeddings, ESM-2 adopted Rotary Position Embeddings (RoPE) following Su et al. (2021), which encode relative positions through rotation matrices applied to query and key vectors. The model was trained across scales from 8M to 15B parameters on UniRef50/UniRef90 clusters. Pre-layer normalization (applying LayerNorm before rather than after attention) was used for training stability at scale. The architecture uses GELU activations in the feed-forward layers. Reference: ESM-2 (Lin et al., Science 2023); RoPE (Su et al., arXiv 2021).
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class ProteinTransformerLayer(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, d_model=320, nhead=20, dim_ff=1280):
        super().__init__()
        self.self_attn = nn.MultiheadAttention(d_model, nhead, batch_first=True)
        self.ff = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(d_model, dim_ff),
            nn.{{b1}}(),
            nn.Linear(dim_ff, d_model)
        )
        # Pre-layer normalization (used in ESM-2)
        self.norm1 = nn.{{b2}}(d_model)
        self.norm2 = nn.LayerNorm(d_model)

    def forward(self, x):
        # Pre-norm: normalize before attention
        normed = self.norm1(x)
        attn_out, _ = self.self_attn(normed, normed, normed)
        x = x + attn_out
        # Pre-norm: normalize before feed-forward
        x = x + self.ff({{b3}})
        return x

layer = ProteinTransformerLayer()
seq = torch.randn(2, 50, 320)  # batch=2, length=50, dim=320
out = layer(seq)
print("Output shape:", out.shape)

Layer normalization is applied before each sub-layer in pre-norm transformers.

questions.title: Autoregressive Protein Sequence Generation
questions.description: Implement top-k sampling for autoregressive protein sequence generation using a GPT-style protein language model like ProtGPT2.
questions.info: ProtGPT2 (Ferruz et al., Nature Communications 2022) is an autoregressive protein language model based on GPT-2 that generates de novo protein sequences by predicting amino acids left to right. Unlike masked models (ESM), autoregressive models learn P(x_t | x_<t) and can generate entirely new sequences by sampling from the predicted distribution. Ferruz et al. showed that generated sequences fold into globular structures and occupy unexplored regions of the protein fitness landscape. Top-k sampling restricts sampling to the k most likely next tokens to avoid low-probability amino acids. ProGen (Madani et al., Nature Biotechnology 2023) extended this approach using conditional generation with control tags for protein family and function. Reference: ProtGPT2 (Ferruz et al., Nature Communications 2022); ProGen (Madani et al., Nature Biotechnology 2023).
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

def top_k_sampling(logits, k=10, temperature=1.0):
    """Sample from the top-k logits with temperature scaling."""
    # Apply temperature
    logits = logits / {{b1}}
    # Get top-k values and indices
    top_k_vals, top_k_idx = torch.topk(logits, k)
    # Zero out everything below top-k
    logits_filtered = torch.full_like(logits, float('-inf'))
    logits_filtered.scatter_(0, top_k_idx, top_k_vals)
    # Convert to probabilities and sample
    probs = F.softmax(logits_filtered, dim=-1)
    sampled_token = torch.{{b2}}(probs, 1)
    return sampled_token

def generate_protein(model, start_token, max_len=100, k=10):
    """Generate a protein sequence autoregressively."""
    tokens = [start_token]
    for _ in range(max_len):
        input_ids = torch.tensor([tokens])
        with torch.no_grad():
            logits = model(input_ids).logits[0, {{b3}}, :]
        next_token = top_k_sampling(logits, k=k)
        tokens.append(next_token.item())
    return tokens

# Demonstration with random logits
logits = torch.randn(25)  # 25 = 20 AAs + specials
sampled = top_k_sampling(logits, k=5)
print("Sampled token index:", sampled.item())

blanks.hint: Divide logits by temperature to control sampling randomness.
Use torch.multinomial to sample from the probability distribution.
Take the logits at the last position (-1) for next token prediction.

questions.title: Perplexity Evaluation for Protein Language Models
questions.description: Compute the perplexity of a protein language model on a set of sequences. Perplexity measures how well the model predicts the observed amino acids.
questions.info: Perplexity is the standard intrinsic evaluation metric for language models, defined as the exponentiation of the average negative log-likelihood per token. For protein LMs, lower perplexity indicates the model better captures the distribution of natural protein sequences. Ferruz et al. (Nature Communications 2022) used perplexity to evaluate ProtGPT2 and showed that generated sequences have perplexity comparable to natural proteins, while random sequences have much higher perplexity. ESM-2 (Lin et al., Science 2023) demonstrated that perplexity decreases with model scale, correlating with improved downstream task performance. Perplexity also serves as a proxy for sequence naturalness in protein design. Reference: ProtGPT2 (Ferruz et al., Nature Communications 2022); ESM-2 (Lin et al., Science 2023).
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

def compute_perplexity(logits, target_ids, ignore_index=-100):
    """Compute perplexity from model logits and targets.
    logits: (batch, seq_len, vocab_size)
    target_ids: (batch, seq_len)
    """
    # Flatten for cross-entropy
    vocab_size = logits.size(-1)
    loss = F.cross_entropy(
        logits.view(-1, vocab_size),
        target_ids.view(-1),
        ignore_index=ignore_index,
        reduction={{b1}}
    )
    perplexity = torch.{{b2}}(loss)
    return perplexity.item()

# Simulated model output
batch_size, seq_len, vocab_size = 4, 50, 33
logits = torch.randn(batch_size, seq_len, vocab_size)
targets = torch.randint(0, vocab_size, (batch_size, seq_len))

ppl = compute_perplexity(logits, targets)
print(f"Perplexity: {ppl:.2f}")
# Random baseline perplexity should be ~vocab_size
random_ppl = {{b3}}
print(f"Random baseline: {random_ppl}")

blanks.hint: Use 'mean' reduction to get the average cross-entropy loss.
Perplexity = exp(average cross-entropy loss).
A random model over V tokens has perplexity equal to the vocabulary size.

questions.title: Transfer Learning: Secondary Structure Prediction
questions.description: Use pre-trained ESM-2 embeddings with a simple token-level classifier for 3-state secondary structure prediction (helix H, strand E, coil C).
questions.info: Secondary structure prediction is a classic benchmark for evaluating protein representations. Rives et al. (PNAS 2021) showed that a linear probe on ESM-1b embeddings achieves ~73% Q3 accuracy, and a small neural network head reaches near state-of-the-art performance. This transfer learning paradigm demonstrates that pre-trained protein LMs capture local structural information without explicit structural supervision. ProtTrans (Elnaggar et al., IEEE TPAMI 2022) compared multiple architectures (BERT, Albert, XLNet, T5, Electra) and showed ProtT5 achieved the best secondary structure prediction accuracy. The task is framed as per-residue classification with three classes: H (alpha-helix), E (beta-strand), and C (coil). Reference: ESM-1b (Rives et al., PNAS 2021); ProtTrans (Elnaggar et al., IEEE TPAMI 2022).
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class SSPredictor(nn.Module):
    """3-state secondary structure predictor using frozen PLM embeddings."""
    def __init__(self, embed_dim=320, num_classes=3):
        super().__init__()
        self.head = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(embed_dim, 128),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Dropout(0.25),
            nn.Linear(128, {{b1}})
        )

    def forward(self, embeddings):
        """embeddings: (batch, seq_len, embed_dim) from frozen PLM"""
        return self.head(embeddings)  # (batch, seq_len, 3)

# Labels: H=0, E=1, C=2
SS_LABELS = {"H": 0, "E": 1, "C": 2}

# Example
model = SSPredictor()
embeddings = torch.randn(2, 50, 320)  # from ESM-2
logits = model(embeddings)

# Per-residue predictions
predictions = torch.{{b2}}(logits, dim=-1)
print("Prediction shape:", predictions.shape)  # (2, 50)

# Loss: per-residue cross-entropy
ss_labels = torch.randint(0, 3, (2, 50))
loss = nn.functional.{{b3}}(
    logits.view(-1, 3), ss_labels.view(-1)
)
print(f"Loss: {loss.item():.4f}")

questions.title: Protein Similarity via Embedding Cosine Distance
questions.description: Compute pairwise similarity between protein sequences using mean-pooled ESM-2 embeddings and cosine similarity.
questions.info: Protein language model embeddings can serve as learned representations for computing sequence similarity that captures more than raw sequence identity. Rives et al. (PNAS 2021) showed that ESM-1b embedding space clusters proteins by structural and functional similarity, even when sequence identity is low (remote homology detection). Mean-pooling over residue embeddings (excluding special tokens) produces a fixed-size sequence representation. Cosine similarity in this space correlates with structural similarity (TM-score) better than sequence-based metrics like BLAST e-values. This approach enables fast, alignment-free comparison of protein sequences. Reference: ESM-1b (Rives et al., PNAS 2021); ESM-2 (Lin et al., Science 2023).
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

def mean_pool(embeddings, attention_mask):
    """Mean pool over sequence length, respecting padding mask."""
    mask = attention_mask.unsqueeze(-1).float()
    summed = (embeddings * mask).sum(dim=1)
    lengths = mask.sum(dim=1)
    return summed / lengths

    """Compute pairwise cosine similarity matrix."""
    # Normalize embeddings
    normed = F.{{b1}}(embeds, dim=-1)
    # Cosine sim = dot product of normalized vectors

    return sim_matrix

# Simulate 5 protein embeddings of dimension 320
embeds = torch.randn(5, 320)
sim = pairwise_cosine_similarity(embeds)
print("Similarity matrix shape:", sim.shape)
print("Diagonal (self-similarity):", {{b3}})

questions.title: Evolutionary Scale: Training Data Curation
questions.description: Implement the UniRef clustering-based data loading strategy used to train ESM models, simulating cluster sampling for training diversity.
questions.info: A key insight behind ESM models is training on evolutionary-scale data. ESM-1b (Rives et al., PNAS 2021) was trained on UniRef50, which clusters UniProt sequences at 50% sequence identity, yielding ~43 million representative sequences. ESM-2 (Lin et al., Science 2023) used a combination of UniRef50 and UniRef90 to balance diversity and coverage, totaling hundreds of millions of sequences. The clustering approach ensures the model sees diverse evolutionary examples rather than being biased toward well-studied protein families. Sampling from clusters rather than individual sequences avoids over-representation of large families. This evolutionary-scale training is what enables the models to learn generalizable protein representations. Reference: ESM-1b (Rives et al., PNAS 2021); ESM-2 (Lin et al., Science 2023).
questions.code:
import random
from collections import defaultdict

class UniRefClusterSampler:
    """Sample protein sequences by first sampling clusters, then
    sampling a representative from each cluster."""

    def __init__(self, clusters: dict):
        """
        clusters: dict mapping cluster_id -> list of sequences
        """
        self.clusters = clusters
        self.cluster_ids = list(clusters.keys())

    def sample_batch(self, batch_size):
        """Sample a batch using cluster-based sampling."""
        # Step 1: sample cluster IDs (with replacement)
        sampled_clusters = random.{{b1}}(self.cluster_ids, k=batch_size)
        # Step 2: pick one random sequence from each cluster
        batch = []
        for cid in sampled_clusters:
            seq = random.{{b2}}(self.clusters[cid])
            batch.append(seq)
        return batch

    @property
    def num_clusters(self):
        return {{b3}}

# Example: 3 clusters with varying sizes
clusters = {
    "C001": ["MKTL", "MKTI", "MKTV"],
    "C002": ["ARND"],
    "C003": ["WYFL", "WYFV"]
}

print(f"Clusters: {sampler.num_clusters}")

questions.title: Conditional Protein Generation with Control Tags
questions.description: Implement the ProGen-style conditional generation approach where control tags (taxonomy, function) are prepended to the protein sequence during training.
questions.info: ProGen (Madani et al., Nature Biotechnology 2023) introduced conditional protein generation by prepending control tags to sequences during autoregressive training. Tags encode protein family, biological process, molecular function, and taxonomic origin from UniProt annotations. At generation time, desired properties are specified through these tags, enabling controllable protein design. The model was trained on 280 million sequences with associated metadata. Madani et al. demonstrated that generated proteins are functional when tested experimentally, with artificial lysozymes showing antimicrobial activity. This tag-conditioning approach allows steering generation toward specific protein families without fine-tuning. Reference: ProGen (Madani et al., Nature Biotechnology 2023).
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class ConditionalProteinLM(nn.Module):
    """Simplified ProGen-style conditional generation."""
    def __init__(self, vocab_size=30, tag_vocab_size=100,
                 d_model=256, nhead=8, num_layers=4):
        super().__init__()
        # Shared embedding for tokens and tags
        total_vocab = vocab_size + tag_vocab_size
        self.embedding = nn.Embedding(total_vocab, d_model)
        decoder_layer = nn.TransformerDecoderLayer(
            d_model=d_model, nhead=nhead, batch_first=True
        )
        self.decoder = nn.TransformerDecoder(decoder_layer, num_layers)
        self.lm_head = nn.Linear(d_model, {{b1}})

    def forward(self, input_ids):
        emb = self.embedding(input_ids)
        seq_len = input_ids.size(1)
        # Causal mask for autoregressive generation
        causal_mask = torch.triu(
            torch.ones(seq_len, seq_len), diagonal=1
        ).bool()
        out = self.decoder(
            emb, emb, tgt_mask=causal_mask
        )
        return self.lm_head(out)

def create_tagged_sequence(tags, sequence, tag_offset=30):
    """Prepend control tags to protein sequence.
    tags: list of tag ids, sequence: list of AA ids
    """
    tagged_tags = [t + {{b2}} for t in tags]
    return tagged_tags + sequence

# Example: taxonomy=5, function=12, then sequence
tagged = create_tagged_sequence([5, 12], [1, 4, 7, 11, 3])
print("Tagged input:", tagged)

model = ConditionalProteinLM()
input_ids = torch.tensor([tagged])
logits = model(input_ids)
next_token_logits = logits[:, {{b3}}, :]
print("Next token logits shape:", next_token_logits.shape)

blanks.hint: The LM head projects to the total vocabulary (tokens + tags).
Add the tag_offset to shift tag IDs into the tag portion of the vocabulary.
In autoregressive models, the next token prediction comes from the last position.

questions.title: Analyzing Attention Head Specialization
questions.description: Analyze which attention heads in a protein language model attend to specific amino acid properties (e.g., hydrophobic residues, charged residues).
questions.info: Vig et al. (ICLR 2021 workshop, 'BERTology Meets Biology') systematically analyzed attention heads in protein transformers and found that specific heads specialize in attending to amino acids sharing biochemical properties. Some heads preferentially attend between hydrophobic residues, others between charged residues, and some capture binding site interactions. This interpretability analysis reveals that protein LMs learn biologically meaningful features without supervision. Rives et al. (PNAS 2021) extended this finding by showing that attention patterns in ESM-1b correlate with protein contact maps. Analyzing attention specialization helps understand what structural and chemical properties are captured by different layers and heads. Reference: Vig et al. (ICLR 2021 workshop); ESM-1b (Rives et al., PNAS 2021).
questions.code:
import torch
import numpy as np

# Define amino acid groups by property
HYDROPHOBIC = set("AILMFWVP")
CHARGED = set("DEKRH")
POLAR = set("STNQYC")

def compute_group_attention(attn_matrix, sequence, group):
    """Compute average attention between residues in a given group.
    attn_matrix: (seq_len, seq_len)
    sequence: string of amino acids
    group: set of amino acid characters
    """
    indices = [i for i, aa in enumerate(sequence) if aa in group]
    if len(indices) < 2:
        return 0.0
    # Extract sub-matrix for group residues
    idx = torch.tensor(indices)
    sub_attn = attn_matrix[{{b1}}][:, idx]
    # Return mean attention within the group
    return sub_attn.{{b2}}().item()

def rank_heads_by_property(attention, sequence, group):
    """Rank attention heads by how much they attend within a group.
    attention: (num_heads, seq_len, seq_len)
    """
    scores = []
    for head_idx in range(attention.shape[0]):
        score = compute_group_attention(
            attention[{{b3}}], sequence, group
        )
        scores.append((head_idx, score))
    return sorted(scores, key=lambda x: -x[1])

seq = "MKTLLILAVVAAALARNDCEQ"
attn = torch.rand(12, len(seq), len(seq))  # 12 heads
ranked = rank_heads_by_property(attn, seq, HYDROPHOBIC)
print("Top 3 heads for hydrophobic attention:")
for head, score in ranked[:3]:
    print(f"  Head {head}: {score:.4f}")

Compute the mean attention value within the sub-matrix.
Index into the first dimension to get a single head's attention map.

questions.title: MLM Training Step for Protein LM
questions.description: Implement a single training step for masked language modeling on protein sequences, including forward pass, loss computation, and backward pass.
questions.info: Training a protein masked language model follows the same procedure as BERT: mask a fraction of input tokens, compute the model's predictions at masked positions, and minimize cross-entropy loss. ESM-1b (Rives et al., PNAS 2021) was trained on 86 billion amino acids from UniRef50 using this objective. The training used the Adam optimizer with a learning rate warmup schedule. ESM-2 (Lin et al., Science 2023) introduced several training improvements including longer training, larger batch sizes, and rotary position embeddings. The loss is only computed at masked positions, so non-masked labels are set to -100 (the default ignore_index for PyTorch's CrossEntropyLoss). Reference: ESM-1b (Rives et al., PNAS 2021); ESM-2 (Lin et al., Science 2023).
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

def train_step(model, optimizer, input_ids, labels):
    """Single MLM training step.
    input_ids: (batch, seq_len) with some positions masked
    labels: (batch, seq_len) with -100 at non-masked positions
    """
    model.train()
    optimizer.zero_grad()

    # Forward pass
    outputs = model(input_ids=input_ids, labels=labels)
    loss = outputs.{{b1}}

    # Backward pass
    loss.{{b2}}

    # Gradient clipping (common for transformer training)
    torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), max_norm=1.0)

    # Update weights
    optimizer.{{b3}}

    return loss.item()

# Example with a small ESM model
from transformers import EsmForMaskedLM
model = EsmForMaskedLM.from_pretrained("facebook/esm2_t6_8M_UR50D")
optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-5)

# Simulated batch
input_ids = torch.randint(4, 24, (2, 30))
labels = input_ids.clone()
labels[labels != 32] = -100  # Only compute loss on masked tokens

loss_val = train_step(model, optimizer, input_ids, labels)
print(f"Training loss: {loss_val:.4f}")

questions.title: Fine-Tuning for Protein Property Regression
questions.description: Fine-tune a PLM with a regression head to predict a continuous protein property (e.g., melting temperature, fluorescence) from the mean-pooled representation.
questions.info: Regression tasks are common in protein engineering, where one needs to predict continuous properties like thermal stability (Tm), fluorescence intensity, or binding affinity. The FLIP benchmark (Dallago et al., NeurIPS 2021 Datasets track) evaluated PLM fine-tuning on multiple such tasks and showed that ESM-1b and ProtTrans representations provide strong starting points for regression. The typical approach mean-pools residue embeddings (excluding special tokens) to obtain a fixed-size vector, then applies a regression head. Spearman correlation is the standard evaluation metric since absolute values matter less than ranking. Freezing the backbone and only training the head (linear probing) often works well with limited labeled data. Reference: FLIP (Dallago et al., NeurIPS 2021); ESM-1b (Rives et al., PNAS 2021).
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class PropertyPredictor(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, plm, hidden_size=320):
        super().__init__()
        self.plm = plm
        # Freeze PLM weights
        for param in self.plm.parameters():
            param.requires_grad = {{b1}}
        self.regressor = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(hidden_size, 128),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Dropout(0.1),
            nn.Linear(128, 1)
        )

    def forward(self, input_ids, attention_mask):
        with torch.no_grad():
            embeddings = self.plm(
                input_ids=input_ids,
                attention_mask=attention_mask
            ).last_hidden_state
        # Mean pool (exclude special tokens: positions 0 and -1)
        mask = attention_mask[:, 1:-1].unsqueeze(-1).float()
        emb = embeddings[:, 1:-1, :]
        pooled = (emb * mask).sum(dim=1) / mask.sum(dim=1)
        return self.regressor(pooled).squeeze(-1)

# Training
from transformers import EsmModel
plm = EsmModel.from_pretrained("facebook/esm2_t6_8M_UR50D")
model = PropertyPredictor(plm)

# MSE loss for regression
criterion = nn.{{b2}}
optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(
    filter(lambda p: p.requires_grad, model.parameters()),
    lr={{b3}}
)
print("Trainable params:",
      sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters() if p.requires_grad))

blanks.hint: Set requires_grad to False to freeze the PLM backbone weights.

questions.title: LoRA Fine-Tuning for Protein LMs
questions.description: Apply Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) to efficiently fine-tune a protein language model with a fraction of the trainable parameters.
questions.info: LoRA (Hu et al., ICLR 2022) enables efficient fine-tuning by injecting trainable low-rank decomposition matrices into transformer layers while keeping pre-trained weights frozen. For protein LMs, this is especially valuable given the large model sizes (ESM-2 15B). The method adds matrices A (d x r) and B (r x d) where r << d, reducing trainable parameters by orders of magnitude. Recent work has applied LoRA to ESM-2 for various protein engineering tasks, achieving comparable performance to full fine-tuning with <1% of trainable parameters. The PEFT library from Hugging Face provides a straightforward implementation. This approach is particularly useful when GPU memory is limited and training data is scarce. Reference: LoRA (Hu et al., ICLR 2022); ESM-2 (Lin et al., Science 2023).
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class LoRALayer(nn.Module):
    """Low-Rank Adaptation layer for efficient fine-tuning."""
    def __init__(self, original_layer, rank=8, alpha=16):
        super().__init__()
        self.original = original_layer
        # Freeze original weights
        for p in self.original.parameters():
            p.requires_grad = False
        d_in = original_layer.in_features
        d_out = original_layer.out_features
        # Low-rank matrices
        self.lora_A = nn.Parameter(torch.randn(d_in, rank) * 0.01)
        self.lora_B = nn.Parameter(torch.{{b1}}(rank, d_out))
        self.scaling = alpha / rank

    def forward(self, x):
        original_out = self.original(x)
        # LoRA: W*x + (x @ A @ B) * scaling
        lora_out = x @ self.lora_A @ {{b2}} * self.scaling
        return original_out + lora_out

def apply_lora(model, rank=8, target_modules=["query", "key"]):
    """Replace target linear layers with LoRA-adapted versions."""
    for name, module in model.named_modules():
        for target in target_modules:
            if target in name and isinstance(module, nn.Linear):
                parent_name = name.rsplit('.', 1)[0]
                parent = dict(model.named_modules())[parent_name]
                attr = name.rsplit('.', 1)[1]
                setattr(parent, attr, LoRALayer(module, rank=rank))

# Count trainable parameters
def count_params(model):
    trainable = sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters() if {{b3}})
    total = sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters())
    return trainable, total

# Example
linear = nn.Linear(320, 320)
lora = LoRALayer(linear, rank=8)
print(f"Original params: {320*320}")
print(f"LoRA params: {320*8 + 8*320} = {320*8*2}")
print(f"Reduction: {320*320 / (320*8*2):.1f}x")

blanks.hint: Initialize lora_B with zeros so that LoRA starts as identity (no change to original model).
Multiply by self.lora_B to complete the low-rank projection A @ B.
Check p.requires_grad to identify trainable parameters.

questions.info: ESM-3 (Hayes et al., Science 2024) introduced a generative masked language model that reasons over protein sequence, structure, and function simultaneously. Each modality is independently tokenized: sequence uses standard amino acid tokens, structure is discretized via a VQ-VAE into 4096 structural tokens per residue, and function is encoded from InterPro annotations. These three token tracks are combined into a unified input representation through learned track-specific embeddings summed together. The model is trained with a masked prediction objective across all tracks, enabling it to generate any modality conditioned on any combination of the others. ESM-3 was trained at scales up to 98 billion parameters on 3.15 billion protein sequences. Reference: Hayes et al. (Science 2024).

class MultiTrackEmbedding(nn.Module):

    sequence, structure, and function token streams."""

        self.struct_embed = nn.Embedding(struct_vocab, d_model)
        self.func_embed = nn.Embedding(func_vocab, d_model)
        self.layer_norm = nn.LayerNorm(d_model)

    def forward(self, seq_tokens, struct_tokens, func_tokens):
        """Combine embeddings from all three tracks."""
        s = self.seq_embed(seq_tokens)
        st = self.struct_embed(struct_tokens)
        f = self.func_embed(func_tokens)
        combined = {{b1}}
        return self.layer_norm(combined)

model = MultiTrackEmbedding()
seq_tok = torch.randint(0, 33, (2, 50))

questions.title: ESM-3 Iterative Masked Generation
questions.description: Implement the iterative decoding strategy used in ESM-3, where generation proceeds by progressively unmasking tokens from a fully masked sequence over multiple steps.
questions.info: ESM-3 (Hayes et al., Science 2024) generates proteins using an iterative unmasking procedure inspired by MaskGIT (Chang et al., CVPR 2022). Starting from a fully masked sequence, at each step the model predicts all masked positions, and a fraction of the most confident predictions are accepted (unmasked) while the rest remain masked. This process repeats for a fixed number of steps T until all positions are unmasked. The scheduling function determines what fraction of tokens to unmask at each step, typically following a cosine or linear schedule. This approach allows the model to capture bidirectional dependencies unlike autoregressive generation. Hayes et al. demonstrated that this strategy can generate functional proteins, including a novel green fluorescent protein (esmGFP). Reference: Hayes et al. (Science 2024); MaskGIT (Chang et al., CVPR 2022).
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

def iterative_unmask(model, seq_len, num_steps=10, vocab_size=33, mask_id=32):
    """ESM-3-style iterative unmasking generation."""
    # Start fully masked
    tokens = torch.full((1, seq_len), mask_id, dtype=torch.long)
    
    for step in range(num_steps):
        with torch.no_grad():
            logits = model(tokens)  # (1, seq_len, vocab_size)
        
        # Get probabilities at masked positions
        mask = (tokens == mask_id).squeeze(0)  # (seq_len,)
        if not mask.any():
            break
        
        probs = F.softmax(logits[0], dim=-1)  # (seq_len, vocab_size)
        # Sample from distribution at each position
        sampled = torch.multinomial(probs.view(-1, vocab_size), 1).squeeze(-1)
        # Get confidence (max prob) at each masked position
        confidence = probs.{{b1}}(dim=-1).values
        confidence[~mask] = -1.0  # ignore already unmasked
        
        # Fraction of remaining masks to unmask this step
        frac = (step + 1) / num_steps
        n_to_unmask = max(1, int(mask.sum().item() * frac))
        
        # Pick top-k most confident masked positions
        _, top_idx = torch.{{b2}}(confidence, n_to_unmask)
        
        # Unmask those positions
        tokens[0, top_idx] = sampled[top_idx]
    
    return tokens

# Dummy model for demonstration
class DummyMLM(torch.nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.linear = torch.nn.Linear(33, 33)
    def forward(self, x):
        one_hot = F.{{b3}}(x, num_classes=33).float()
        return self.linear(one_hot)

model = DummyMLM()
result = iterative_unmask(model, seq_len=20, num_steps=5)
print("Generated tokens:", result)

blanks.hint: Use .max(dim=-1) to get the highest probability (confidence) at each position.
Use torch.topk to select the most confident positions to unmask.
Use F.one_hot to convert token indices to one-hot vectors.

questions.title: Protein Sequence Clustering with PLM Embeddings
questions.description: Cluster protein sequences by computing mean-pooled PLM embeddings and applying k-means clustering, then evaluate cluster quality with silhouette score.
questions.info: Traditional protein sequence clustering tools like CD-HIT (Li & Godzik, Bioinformatics 2006) and MMseqs2 (Steinegger & Soding, Nature Biotechnology 2017) rely on sequence identity thresholds. PLM embeddings offer an alternative that captures functional and structural similarity beyond raw sequence identity. Rives et al. (PNAS 2021) showed that ESM-1b embedding space naturally clusters proteins by structural fold even at low sequence identity (<25%). Clustering in embedding space can uncover remote homology relationships missed by sequence-based methods. The silhouette score measures how similar a point is to its own cluster versus neighboring clusters, ranging from -1 to 1. Higher values indicate better-defined clusters. Reference: ESM-1b (Rives et al., PNAS 2021); MMseqs2 (Steinegger & Soding, Nature Biotechnology 2017).
questions.code:
import torch
from sklearn.cluster import KMeans
from sklearn.metrics import silhouette_score
import numpy as np

def cluster_proteins(embeddings, n_clusters=5):
    """Cluster proteins using k-means on PLM embeddings.
    embeddings: (N, embed_dim) numpy array
    """
    kmeans = KMeans(n_clusters=n_clusters, random_state=42, n_init=10)
    labels = kmeans.{{b1}}(embeddings)
    score = silhouette_score(embeddings, labels)
    return labels, score, kmeans

# Simulate 100 protein embeddings of dimension 320
np.random.seed(42)
embeddings = np.random.randn(100, 320).astype(np.float32)

labels, sil_score, km = cluster_proteins(embeddings, n_clusters=5)
print(f"Silhouette score: {sil_score:.4f}")
print(f"Cluster sizes: {np.{{b2}}(labels)}")

# Get cluster centroids
centroids = km.{{b3}}
print(f"Centroid shape: {centroids.shape}")

questions.title: MSA Transformer Tied Row Attention
questions.description: Implement the tied row attention mechanism from the MSA Transformer, where attention weights are shared (tied) across sequences in a multiple sequence alignment.
questions.info: The MSA Transformer (Rao et al., ICML 2021) processes multiple sequence alignments as 2D inputs with row (per-sequence) and column (per-position) attention. The key innovation is tied row attention, where the attention weights are computed by averaging queries and keys across all sequences in the MSA before computing attention. This forces the model to learn position-level patterns that are consistent across all sequences in the alignment. Column attention operates across sequences at each position, capturing coevolutionary signals. The MSA Transformer achieved state-of-the-art unsupervised contact prediction, outperforming single-sequence models. It was trained on MSAs derived from UniRef50 clusters. Reference: Rao et al. (ICML 2021).
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
import math

class TiedRowAttention(nn.Module):
    """Tied row attention: attention weights shared across MSA rows."""
    def __init__(self, d_model=256, nhead=8):
        super().__init__()
        self.nhead = nhead
        self.d_k = d_model // nhead
        self.W_q = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model)
        self.W_k = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model)
        self.W_v = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model)
        self.W_o = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model)

    def forward(self, x):
        """x: (batch, num_seqs, seq_len, d_model)"""
        B, M, L, D = x.shape
        # Project Q, K, V
        Q = self.W_q(x).view(B, M, L, self.nhead, self.d_k)
        K = self.W_k(x).view(B, M, L, self.nhead, self.d_k)
        V = self.W_v(x).view(B, M, L, self.nhead, self.d_k)
        
        # Tied: average Q and K across MSA rows (dim=1)
        Q_tied = Q.{{b1}}(dim=1, keepdim=True).expand_as(Q)
        K_tied = K.mean(dim=1, keepdim=True).expand_as(K)
        
        # Reshape for attention: (B*M, nhead, L, d_k)
        Q_tied = Q_tied.reshape(B*M, L, self.nhead, self.d_k).transpose(1, 2)
        K_tied = K_tied.reshape(B*M, L, self.nhead, self.d_k).transpose(1, 2)
        V = V.reshape(B*M, L, self.nhead, self.d_k).transpose(1, 2)
        
        # Scaled dot-product attention
        scores = (Q_tied @ K_tied.transpose(-2, -1)) / math.sqrt(self.d_k)
        attn = F.{{b2}}(scores, dim=-1)
        out = (attn @ V).transpose(1, 2).reshape(B, M, L, D)
        return self.W_o(out)

# Test: batch=1, 8 sequences, length 30, dim 256
x = torch.randn(1, 8, 30, 256)
layer = TiedRowAttention(d_model=256, nhead={{b3}})
out = layer(x)
print("Output shape:", out.shape)  # (1, 8, 30, 256)

blanks.hint: Use .mean() to average queries across all sequences in the MSA.
Apply softmax to convert scores to attention weights.
Use 8 attention heads to match d_model=256 (256/8=32 per head).

questions.title: Contrastive Learning for Protein Representations
questions.description: Implement an NT-Xent (Normalized Temperature-scaled Cross Entropy) contrastive loss for learning protein representations, where augmented views of the same protein form positive pairs.
questions.info: Contrastive learning has been applied to proteins to learn representations that group functionally similar proteins together. Lu et al. (2022, 'Contrastive learning on protein embeddings') showed that applying contrastive objectives on top of PLM embeddings improves protein function prediction. Positive pairs are typically constructed by taking two augmented views of the same protein (e.g., subsequences, masking, or homologous sequences). The NT-Xent loss from SimCLR (Chen et al., ICML 2020) is commonly used, which maximizes agreement between positive pairs while pushing apart negatives within the batch. A temperature parameter controls the sharpness of the similarity distribution. This approach is especially useful for learning representations when labeled data is scarce. Reference: SimCLR (Chen et al., ICML 2020); Lu et al. (Bioinformatics 2022).
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

def nt_xent_loss(z1, z2, temperature=0.1):
    """NT-Xent contrastive loss for protein embedding pairs.
    z1, z2: (batch, embed_dim) - two views of the same proteins
    """
    batch_size = z1.size(0)
    # Normalize embeddings
    z1 = F.normalize(z1, dim=-1)
    z2 = F.normalize(z2, dim=-1)
    
    # Concatenate both views
    z = torch.cat([z1, z2], dim=0)  # (2*batch, dim)
    
    # Pairwise cosine similarity
    sim = z @ z.T / {{b1}}
    
    # Mask out self-similarity (diagonal)
    mask = torch.eye(2 * batch_size, dtype=torch.bool)
    sim.masked_fill_(mask, {{b2}})
    
    # Positive pairs: (i, i+batch) and (i+batch, i)
    labels = torch.cat([
        torch.arange(batch_size, 2 * batch_size),
        torch.arange(0, batch_size)
    ])
    
    loss = F.{{b3}}(sim, labels)
    return loss

# Test with batch of 16 protein embeddings
z1 = torch.randn(16, 320)  # view 1
z2 = torch.randn(16, 320)  # view 2 (augmented)
loss = nt_xent_loss(z1, z2, temperature=0.1)
print(f"Contrastive loss: {loss.item():.4f}")

blanks.hint: Divide similarities by the temperature parameter to control distribution sharpness.
Set self-similarity to negative infinity so it becomes zero after softmax.
Use cross_entropy loss with the positive pair indices as labels.

questions.title: ProtST: Protein-Text Dual Encoder
questions.description: Implement a dual-encoder model inspired by ProtST that aligns protein sequence embeddings with natural language function descriptions in a shared embedding space.
questions.info: ProtST (Xu et al., ICML 2023) introduced a framework for learning protein representations aligned with biomedical text descriptions. The model uses a protein encoder (based on ESM) and a text encoder (based on PubMedBERT) trained with contrastive and matching objectives. Protein-text pairs are derived from Swiss-Prot entries where each protein has a textual function annotation. The contrastive objective pulls matching protein-text pairs together while pushing non-matching pairs apart in the shared embedding space. ProtST demonstrated that text-supervised protein representations improve zero-shot protein classification and retrieval tasks. The approach bridges protein sequence space and natural language, enabling text-based protein search. Reference: Xu et al. (ICML 2023); ESM-2 (Lin et al., Science 2023).
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

class ProteinTextDualEncoder(nn.Module):
    """ProtST-style dual encoder for protein-text alignment."""
    def __init__(self, prot_dim=320, text_dim=768, proj_dim=256):
        super().__init__()
        self.prot_proj = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(prot_dim, proj_dim),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(proj_dim, proj_dim)
        )
        self.text_proj = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(text_dim, proj_dim),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(proj_dim, proj_dim)
        )
        self.temperature = nn.Parameter(torch.tensor(0.07))

    def forward(self, prot_emb, text_emb):
        """Compute contrastive loss between protein and text embeddings."""
        # Project to shared space
        p = F.normalize(self.prot_proj(prot_emb), dim=-1)
        t = F.normalize(self.text_proj(text_emb), dim=-1)
        
        # Similarity matrix
        logits = (p @ t.T) / self.temperature.{{b1}}()
        
        # Symmetric contrastive loss
        batch_size = p.size(0)
        labels = torch.{{b2}}(batch_size, device=p.device)
        loss_p2t = F.cross_entropy(logits, labels)
        loss_t2p = F.cross_entropy(logits.T, labels)
        return (loss_p2t + loss_t2p) / {{b3}}

model = ProteinTextDualEncoder()
prot_emb = torch.randn(8, 320)   # 8 protein embeddings
text_emb = torch.randn(8, 768)   # 8 text embeddings
loss = model(prot_emb, text_emb)
print(f"ProtST-style loss: {loss.item():.4f}")

blanks.hint: Use .exp() to ensure the temperature is always positive via log-parameterization.
Use torch.arange to create labels [0,1,...,N-1] where each protein matches its paired text.
Average the two directional losses by dividing by 2.

questions.title: Instruction-Tuned Protein Language Model
questions.description: Implement an instruction-following wrapper around a protein LM that formats diverse protein tasks (function prediction, localization, etc.) as text instructions paired with protein sequences.
questions.info: InstructProtein (Wang et al., 2024) and Prot2Text (Abdine et al., 2024) adapted instruction-tuning paradigms from NLP to protein language models. These models take natural language instructions alongside protein sequences and generate textual answers about protein properties. The approach unifies multiple protein tasks (function prediction, localization, family classification) under a single generative model conditioned on task-specific instructions. Training data is constructed from Swiss-Prot annotations formatted as instruction-response pairs. This paradigm enables zero-shot generalization to new protein analysis tasks described in natural language. The architecture typically combines a frozen protein encoder with a language model decoder connected via a projection layer. Reference: InstructProtein (Wang et al., 2024); Prot2Text (Abdine et al., 2024).
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class InstructProteinModel(nn.Module):
    """Simplified instruction-tuned protein model."""
    def __init__(self, prot_dim=320, text_dim=512, vocab_size=30000):
        super().__init__()
        # Projection from protein space to text decoder space
        self.prot_projector = nn.Linear(prot_dim, text_dim)
        # Instruction embedding
        self.text_embed = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, text_dim)
        # Simple decoder
        decoder_layer = nn.TransformerDecoderLayer(
            d_model=text_dim, nhead=8, batch_first=True
        )
        self.decoder = nn.TransformerDecoder(decoder_layer, num_layers=2)
        self.lm_head = nn.Linear(text_dim, vocab_size)

    def forward(self, prot_emb, instruction_ids, target_ids=None):
        # Project protein embeddings to text space
        prot_features = self.prot_projector(prot_emb)  # (B, L, text_dim)
        # Embed instruction tokens
        instr_emb = self.text_embed(instruction_ids)
        # Concatenate protein features and instruction as memory
        memory = torch.cat([prot_features, instr_emb], dim={{b1}})
        # Decode (teacher forcing with target)
        if target_ids is not None:
            tgt_emb = self.text_embed(target_ids)
            out = self.decoder(tgt_emb, memory)
            return self.lm_head(out)
        return memory

def format_instruction(task, sequence):
    """Format a protein task as an instruction string."""
    templates = {
        "function": f"What is the function of this protein: {sequence}?",
        "localization": f"Predict the subcellular localization of: {sequence}",
        "family": f"What protein family does this belong to: {sequence}?"
    }
    return templates.get(task, f"Analyze this protein: {sequence}")

model = InstructProteinModel()
prot_emb = torch.randn(2, 10, {{b2}})  # batch=2, 10 residues
instr_ids = torch.randint(0, 30000, (2, 15))
target_ids = torch.randint(0, 30000, (2, 20))
logits = model(prot_emb, instr_ids, target_ids)
print("Output logits shape:", logits.shape)

# Format example
instr = format_instruction("function", "MKTLLILAVV")
print("Instruction:", {{b3}})

blanks.hint: Concatenate along dim=1 (sequence length) to combine protein and instruction tokens.
Match the prot_dim=320 parameter used in the model constructor.
Print the formatted instruction string stored in the 'instr' variable.

questions.title: Protein Sequence Alignment Scoring with LMs
questions.description: Use a protein language model to score pairwise sequence alignments by summing per-position log-likelihoods, providing an alternative to substitution matrix-based scoring (BLOSUM).
questions.info: Protein language models can replace traditional substitution matrices (BLOSUM, PAM) for alignment scoring. Sgarbossa et al. (NAR 2023) showed that ESM-based scoring functions improve remote homology detection in pairwise alignments. The idea is to score each aligned position by the log-probability the LM assigns to the observed amino acid given its context, rather than using a fixed substitution score. Positions where the LM assigns high probability correspond to conserved or structurally important residues. Gap penalties can also be learned from the model's insertion probabilities. This approach captures position-specific and context-dependent substitution preferences that static matrices cannot. Reference: Sgarbossa et al. (NAR 2023); ESM-2 (Lin et al., Science 2023).
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

def lm_alignment_score(log_probs, seq1_ids, seq2_ids,
                       alignment, gap_penalty=-2.0):
    """Score a pairwise alignment using LM log-probabilities.
    log_probs: (seq_len, vocab_size) from masked LM
    alignment: list of tuples (i, j) or (i, -1) for gaps
    """
    score = 0.0
    for pos1, pos2 in alignment:
        if pos1 == -1 or pos2 == -1:
            score += gap_penalty
        else:
            # LM score: log P(aa at pos) from context
            score += log_probs[pos1, seq2_ids[pos2]].item()
    return score

def compute_lm_log_probs(logits):
    """Convert raw logits to log probabilities."""
    return F.{{b1}}(logits, dim=-1)

# Simulated: 30 positions, vocab_size=33
logits = torch.randn(30, 33)
log_probs = compute_lm_log_probs(logits)

# Simple alignment: positions match 1:1 for first 10
alignment = [(i, i) for i in range(10)] + [(-1, 10)]  # one gap
seq1_ids = torch.randint(4, 24, (30,))
seq2_ids = torch.randint(4, 24, (30,))

score = lm_alignment_score(log_probs, seq1_ids, seq2_ids, alignment)
print(f"LM alignment score: {score:.4f}")

# Compare: BLOSUM-like fixed score
blosum_score = sum([{{b2}} for _ in range(10)]) + (-2.0)
print(f"Fixed substitution score: {blosum_score:.4f}")

# The LM score is context-{{b3}}
print("LM scoring is context-dependent unlike BLOSUM")

blanks.hint: Use F.log_softmax to convert logits to log probabilities.
A fixed positive score (e.g., 1 or 2) for each matched position in a simple substitution scheme.
LM scoring is context-dependent, meaning scores change based on surrounding residues.

questions.title: Evolutionary Coupling Analysis from PLM Attention
questions.description: Extract evolutionary couplings (coevolution signals) from a protein language model by analyzing the attention weight patterns across layers and heads, using APC correction.
questions.info: Evolutionary couplings between residue positions arise when mutations at one position are compensated by mutations at another to maintain protein fitness. Traditional methods like Direct Coupling Analysis (DCA) and EVcouplings (Marks et al., PLoS ONE 2011) infer these from MSA covariance statistics. Rao et al. (NeurIPS 2020) showed that transformer attention heads in protein LMs capture coevolutionary signals without explicit MSA input. The model implicitly learns coupling patterns from the training corpus of millions of evolutionary sequences. Combining attention maps from multiple layers and heads using a learned linear combination outperforms using any single head. APC (Average Product Correction) removes background coupling noise. Reference: Marks et al. (PLoS ONE 2011); Rao et al. (NeurIPS 2020).
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

def extract_couplings(attentions, layer_weights=None):
    """Extract evolutionary couplings from multi-layer attention.
    attentions: list of (num_heads, L, L) tensors per layer
    layer_weights: (num_layers, num_heads) learned combination weights
    """
    num_layers = len(attentions)
    num_heads = attentions[0].shape[0]
    L = attentions[0].shape[1]
    
    if layer_weights is None:
        layer_weights = torch.ones(num_layers, num_heads)
    
    coupling_map = torch.zeros(L, L)
    for l, attn in enumerate(attentions):
        for h in range(num_heads):
            w = layer_weights[l, h]
            # Symmetrize each head's attention
            sym = (attn[h] + attn[h].T) / 2
            coupling_map += w * sym
    
    # Apply APC correction
    row_mean = coupling_map.mean(dim={{b1}}, keepdim=True)
    col_mean = coupling_map.mean(dim=0, keepdim=True)
    global_mean = coupling_map.mean()
    corrected = coupling_map - (row_mean * col_mean) / global_mean
    
    # Zero out diagonal (self-contacts not meaningful)
    corrected.fill_diagonal_({{b2}})
    return corrected

# Simulate 6 layers, 12 heads, sequence length 40
attentions = [torch.rand(12, 40, 40) for _ in range(6)]
couplings = extract_couplings(attentions)

# Top predicted couplings
flat_idx = torch.{{b3}}(couplings)
top_k = 5
for i in range(top_k):
    row = flat_idx[i] // 40
    col = flat_idx[i] % 40
    print(f"Coupling {row.item()}-{col.item()}: {couplings[row, col]:.4f}")

blanks.hint: Compute the mean along dim=1 (columns) to get the row mean.
Set diagonal to 0 since self-couplings are not meaningful.

questions.info: Protein family databases like Pfam (Mistry et al., NAR 2021) organize proteins into evolutionary families based on sequence and structural similarity. Traditional family assignment uses profile HMMs (HMMER), but PLM embeddings offer a fast alternative. Bileschi et al. (Nature Biotechnology 2022, ProtCNN/ProtENN) showed that neural networks can classify proteins into 17,929 Pfam families with high accuracy. Using frozen ESM-2 embeddings with a simple MLP head achieves comparable results with minimal training. The <cls> token from ESM-2 serves as a compact whole-sequence representation suitable for family-level classification. This approach is orders of magnitude faster than HMM-based searches at inference time. Reference: Pfam (Mistry et al., NAR 2021); Bileschi et al. (Nature Biotechnology 2022).

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

class PfamClassifier(nn.Module):
    """Classify proteins into Pfam families from PLM embeddings."""
    def __init__(self, embed_dim=320, num_families=1000, dropout=0.3):
        super().__init__()
        self.classifier = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(embed_dim, 512),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.{{b1}}(dropout),
            nn.Linear(512, 256),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Dropout(dropout),
            nn.Linear(256, num_families)
        )

    def forward(self, cls_embedding):
        """cls_embedding: (batch, embed_dim) from ESM-2 <cls> token."""
        return self.classifier(cls_embedding)

model = PfamClassifier(embed_dim=320, num_families=1000)

questions.title: Protein Domain Detection with Token-Level Predictions
questions.description: Implement a token-level domain boundary detection model that labels each residue as belonging to a specific domain or a linker region, using PLM per-residue embeddings.
questions.info: Protein domains are independently folding structural units, and detecting domain boundaries is essential for understanding multidomain proteins. Traditional tools like Pfam (Mistry et al., NAR 2021) and InterPro (Blum et al., NAR 2021) use profile HMMs for domain annotation. Recent work has shown that PLM per-residue embeddings encode domain boundary information. The task is formulated as a sequence labeling (BIO tagging) problem where each residue is labeled as Beginning, Inside, or Outside of a domain. Elnaggar et al. (IEEE TPAMI 2022) showed that ProtTrans embeddings combined with a CRF layer can accurately predict domain boundaries. This approach generalizes better to novel domain architectures than HMM-based methods. Reference: InterPro (Blum et al., NAR 2021); ProtTrans (Elnaggar et al., IEEE TPAMI 2022).
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class DomainDetector(nn.Module):
    """Detect protein domains using BIO tagging on PLM embeddings."""
    def __init__(self, embed_dim=320, hidden_dim=128,
                 num_domain_types=50):
        super().__init__()
        # Total tags: B-domain, I-domain for each type + O (linker)
        self.num_tags = num_domain_types * 2 + 1
        self.bilstm = nn.LSTM(
            embed_dim, hidden_dim, num_layers=1,
            bidirectional={{b1}}, batch_first=True
        )
        self.classifier = nn.Linear(hidden_dim * 2, self.num_tags)

    def forward(self, embeddings):
        """embeddings: (batch, seq_len, embed_dim)"""
        lstm_out, _ = self.bilstm(embeddings)
        logits = self.classifier(lstm_out)
        return logits

    def decode(self, logits):
        """Greedy decode: get most likely tag per residue."""
        return torch.argmax(logits, dim={{b2}})

model = DomainDetector()
embeddings = torch.randn(4, 200, 320)  # batch=4, 200 residues
logits = model(embeddings)
tags = model.decode(logits)
print("Tag shape:", tags.shape)  # (4, 200)
print("Num unique tags:", tags.{{b3}}().numel())

Take argmax along the last dimension (tag dimension) to get predicted tags.
Use .unique() to get the set of distinct predicted tags.

questions.title: Signal Peptide Prediction with PLM
questions.description: Build a signal peptide predictor that classifies the N-terminal region of a protein as having a signal peptide or not, and predicts the cleavage site position.
questions.info: Signal peptides are short N-terminal sequences (typically 15-30 residues) that direct proteins to the secretory pathway. SignalP 6.0 (Teufel et al., Nature Biotechnology 2022) used protein language model embeddings to achieve state-of-the-art signal peptide prediction across all domains of life. The model predicts per-residue labels indicating the n-region, h-region, c-region of the signal peptide and the cleavage site. Combining ESM-2 or ProtTrans embeddings with a conditional random field (CRF) decoder captures the sequential nature of signal peptide regions. The cleavage site typically occurs after the pattern Ala-X-Ala. This is a critical bioinformatics task for protein secretion studies and recombinant protein production. Reference: SignalP 6.0 (Teufel et al., Nature Biotechnology 2022); ESM-2 (Lin et al., Science 2023).
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class SignalPeptidePredictor(nn.Module):
    """Predict signal peptide presence and cleavage site."""
    def __init__(self, embed_dim=320, max_sp_len=70):
        super().__init__()
        # Binary: has signal peptide or not
        self.sp_classifier = nn.Linear(embed_dim, 2)
        # Per-residue: n-region, h-region, c-region, mature
        self.region_classifier = nn.Linear(embed_dim, 4)

    def forward(self, embeddings):
        """embeddings: (batch, seq_len, embed_dim)"""
        # Sequence-level: use mean of first 70 residues
        n_terminal = embeddings[:, :70, :].{{b1}}(dim=1)
        sp_logits = self.sp_classifier(n_terminal)  # (batch, 2)
        # Residue-level region prediction
        region_logits = self.region_classifier(embeddings)
        return sp_logits, region_logits

    def predict_cleavage_site(self, region_logits):
        """Find cleavage site: last position predicted as c-region."""
        # Region labels: 0=n-region, 1=h-region, 2=c-region, 3=mature
        predictions = torch.argmax(region_logits, dim=-1)  # (batch, L)
        # Cleavage site = first position where prediction switches to mature (3)
        is_mature = (predictions == {{b2}})
        # Find first mature position per sequence
        batch_size = predictions.size(0)
        sites = []
        for i in range(batch_size):
            mature_pos = is_mature[i].nonzero(as_tuple=True)[0]
            site = mature_pos[0].item() if len(mature_pos) > 0 else -1
            sites.append(site)
        return sites

model = SignalPeptidePredictor()
emb = torch.randn(2, 100, 320)
sp_logits, region_logits = model(emb)
has_sp = torch.argmax(sp_logits, dim={{b3}})
print("Has signal peptide:", has_sp.tolist())
sites = model.predict_cleavage_site(region_logits)
print("Cleavage sites:", sites)

blanks.hint: Use .mean(dim=1) to average over the sequence length dimension.
The mature protein region is labeled as 3 (after n=0, h=1, c=2).
Take argmax along the last dimension to get binary prediction.

    "Extracellular", "Plastid"

questions.title: Post-Translational Modification Prediction
questions.description: Build a per-residue predictor for post-translational modifications (phosphorylation, glycosylation, ubiquitination) using PLM embeddings with a multi-label binary classification head.
questions.info: Post-translational modifications (PTMs) regulate protein function, and predicting modification sites from sequence is an active area. MusiteDeep (Wang et al., Briefings in Bioinformatics 2020) used deep learning for PTM site prediction across 13 modification types. Recent approaches integrate PLM embeddings for improved accuracy: the local sequence context captured by ESM-2 embeddings is highly informative for PTM sites. The task is per-residue multi-label classification since a single residue can carry multiple modifications. For phosphorylation, only S, T, and Y residues are candidate sites; for glycosylation, N (in N-X-S/T motifs) and S/T are candidates. A sigmoid activation with binary cross-entropy loss handles the multi-label nature. Reference: MusiteDeep (Wang et al., Briefings in Bioinformatics 2020); ESM-2 (Lin et al., Science 2023).
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

PTM_TYPES = ["Phosphorylation", "Glycosylation", "Ubiquitination",
             "Acetylation", "Methylation", "SUMOylation"]

class PTMPredictor(nn.Module):
    """Per-residue multi-label PTM site prediction."""
    def __init__(self, embed_dim=320, num_ptm_types=6, window=7):
        super().__init__()
        # 1D convolution for local context
        self.conv = nn.Conv1d(
            embed_dim, 128, kernel_size=window,
            padding=window // 2
        )
        self.head = nn.Sequential(
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(128, num_ptm_types)
        )

    def forward(self, embeddings):
        """embeddings: (batch, seq_len, embed_dim)"""
        # Conv expects (batch, channels, length)
        x = embeddings.{{b1}}(1, 2)
        x = self.conv(x).transpose(1, 2)  # back to (batch, L, 128)
        logits = self.head(x)  # (batch, L, num_ptm_types)
        return logits

    def predict(self, logits, threshold=0.5):
        """Multi-label prediction using sigmoid threshold."""
        probs = torch.{{b2}}(logits)
        return (probs > threshold).long()

model = PTMPredictor()
emb = torch.randn(4, 100, 320)
logits = model(emb)
predictions = model.predict(logits)
print("Prediction shape:", predictions.shape)  # (4, 100, 6)

# Multi-label BCE loss
targets = torch.zeros(4, 100, 6)
targets[0, 10, 0] = 1  # phosphorylation at position 10
loss = nn.functional.{{b3}}(
    logits, targets
)
print(f"BCE loss: {loss.item():.4f}")

questions.title: Multi-Species Protein LM with Taxonomy Conditioning
questions.description: Implement a protein language model that conditions on species taxonomy information through learned taxonomy embeddings added to the sequence representation, enabling species-aware predictions.
questions.info: Protein language models trained on multi-species data must account for evolutionary divergence between organisms. ProGen2 (Nijkamp et al., Cell Systems 2023) trained models on proteins from diverse taxa and showed improved generation when conditioned on taxonomic information. Species-specific biases in amino acid usage (codon bias), protein length distributions, and functional repertoires mean that taxonomy-aware models can better capture organism-specific sequence patterns. The NCBI taxonomy tree provides hierarchical classification (kingdom, phylum, class, order, family, genus, species) that can be encoded as embeddings at multiple levels. Adding taxonomy embeddings to the sequence representation enables the model to learn species-specific protein distributions. Reference: ProGen2 (Nijkamp et al., Cell Systems 2023); UniProt Consortium (NAR 2023).
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class TaxonomyAwarePLM(nn.Module):
    """Protein LM with taxonomy conditioning."""
    def __init__(self, vocab_size=33, d_model=256, nhead=8,
                 num_layers=4, num_species=10000):
        super().__init__()
        self.aa_embed = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, d_model)
        self.tax_embed = nn.Embedding(num_species, d_model)
        self.pos_embed = nn.Embedding(1024, d_model)
        
        encoder_layer = nn.TransformerEncoderLayer(
            d_model=d_model, nhead=nhead, batch_first=True
        )
        self.encoder = nn.TransformerEncoder(
            encoder_layer, num_layers=num_layers
        )
        self.lm_head = nn.Linear(d_model, vocab_size)

    def forward(self, token_ids, species_id):
        """token_ids: (B, L), species_id: (B,)"""
        seq_len = token_ids.size(1)
        positions = torch.arange(seq_len, device=token_ids.device)
        
        # Combine sequence, position, and taxonomy embeddings
        x = self.aa_embed(token_ids) + self.pos_embed(positions)
        # Broadcast species embedding across all positions
        tax = self.tax_embed(species_id).{{b1}}(1)
        x = x + tax
        
        encoded = self.encoder(x)
        logits = self.lm_head(encoded)
        return logits

model = TaxonomyAwarePLM()
tokens = torch.randint(0, 33, (4, 50))
species = torch.randint(0, 10000, (4,))

logits = model(tokens, species)
print("Logits shape:", logits.shape)  # (4, 50, 33)

# Compare predictions for same sequence, different species
seq = torch.randint(0, 33, (1, 30))
human = torch.tensor([{{b2}}])
yeast = torch.tensor([42])
logits_h = model(seq, human)
logits_y = model(seq, yeast)
diff = (logits_h - logits_y).{{b3}}().mean().item()
print(f"Mean absolute difference between species: {diff:.4f}")

blanks.hint: Use .unsqueeze(1) to add a sequence length dimension for broadcasting.
Use any integer species ID (e.g., 0 or 9606 for human NCBI taxon).
Use .abs() to compute the absolute difference.

questions.title: De Novo Protein Sequence Design with PLM Scoring
questions.description: Implement a Monte Carlo-based protein sequence design method that uses a PLM's pseudo-likelihood as the optimization objective to design sequences predicted to fold into a target structure.
questions.info: Protein language models provide a learned fitness landscape that can guide de novo sequence design. Hie et al. (Nature Biotechnology 2024) used PLM likelihood scores to design functional proteins through directed evolution in silico. The pseudo-likelihood of a sequence under a masked LM sums the log-probabilities of each residue given all others, providing a differentiable fitness score. Metropolis-Hastings sampling with PLM scoring allows exploration of sequence space while maintaining high predicted fitness. Ferruz et al. (Nature Communications 2022) showed that ProtGPT2-generated sequences adopt natural-like structures. The acceptance criterion in MCMC ensures detailed balance: mutations increasing PLM likelihood are always accepted, while decreasing mutations are accepted with probability proportional to exp(-delta/T). Reference: Hie et al. (Nature Biotechnology 2024); Ferruz et al. (Nature Communications 2022).
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F
import random

def pseudo_log_likelihood(model, tokenizer, sequence):
    """Compute pseudo-log-likelihood by masking each position."""
    inputs = tokenizer(sequence, return_tensors="pt")
    token_ids = inputs["input_ids"][0]
    pll = 0.0
    for i in range(1, len(token_ids) - 1):  # skip <cls> and <eos>
        masked = token_ids.clone().unsqueeze(0)
        masked[0, i] = tokenizer.mask_token_id
        with torch.no_grad():
            logits = model(masked).logits[0, i]
        log_probs = F.log_softmax(logits, dim=-1)
        pll += log_probs[token_ids[i]].item()
    return pll

def mcmc_design(model, tokenizer, init_seq, n_steps=100,
                temperature=1.0, vocab="ACDEFGHIKLMNPQRSTVWY"):
    """MCMC protein design using PLM pseudo-likelihood."""
    current_seq = list(init_seq)
    current_pll = pseudo_log_likelihood(
        model, tokenizer, "".join(current_seq)
    )
    best_seq = current_seq[:]
    best_pll = current_pll

    for step in range(n_steps):
        # Propose: mutate one random position
        pos = random.randint(0, len(current_seq) - 1)
        new_aa = random.choice(vocab)
        proposed = current_seq[:]
        proposed[pos] = new_aa
        proposed_pll = pseudo_log_likelihood(
            model, tokenizer, "".join(proposed)
        )
        # Metropolis criterion
        delta = proposed_pll - current_pll
        if delta > 0 or random.random() < torch.{{b1}}(
            torch.tensor(delta / temperature)
        ).item():
            current_seq = proposed
            current_pll = proposed_pll
        if current_pll > {{b2}}:
            best_seq = current_seq[:]
            best_pll = current_pll

    return "".join(best_seq), best_pll

# Demo with dummy values
print("MCMC design uses pseudo-log-likelihood as fitness")
print("Acceptance probability: exp(delta_PLL / T) for delta < {{b3}}")

blanks.hint: Use torch.exp to compute the Metropolis acceptance probability.
Update best if current pseudo-log-likelihood exceeds best_pll.
When delta < 0 (fitness decreases), acceptance is probabilistic.

questions.title: Inverse Folding: Sequence Design from Structure
questions.description: Implement a simplified inverse folding model that takes protein backbone coordinates and predicts a sequence likely to fold into that structure, using a graph neural network over residue neighbors.
questions.info: Inverse folding (also called fixed-backbone design) predicts amino acid sequences that fold into a given 3D structure. ESM-IF1 (Hsu et al., ICML 2022) used a GVP-Transformer architecture conditioned on backbone coordinates to achieve state-of-the-art sequence recovery rates (~51% on CATH). ProteinMPNN (Dauparas et al., Science 2022) used a message-passing neural network approach and demonstrated that designed sequences express and fold experimentally. The key insight is encoding the local geometric environment of each residue (distances and orientations to neighbors) as features for an autoregressive sequence decoder. LM-Design (Zheng et al., ICML 2023) further improved inverse folding by integrating ESM-2 as a sequence prior. The model conditions on Ca-Ca distances and backbone dihedral angles. Reference: ESM-IF1 (Hsu et al., ICML 2022); ProteinMPNN (Dauparas et al., Science 2022).
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

class SimpleInverseFolding(nn.Module):
    """Simplified inverse folding from Ca coordinates."""
    def __init__(self, d_model=128, vocab_size=20, k_neighbors=30):
        super().__init__()
        self.k = k_neighbors
        # Encode pairwise distances and direction
        self.edge_encoder = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(4, 64),  # dist + 3D unit vector
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(64, d_model)
        )
        self.node_update = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(d_model * 2, d_model),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(d_model, d_model)
        )
        self.decoder = nn.Linear(d_model, vocab_size)

    def forward(self, coords):
        """coords: (batch, L, 3) Ca coordinates."""
        B, L, _ = coords.shape
        # Compute pairwise distances
        diff = coords.unsqueeze(2) - coords.unsqueeze(1)  # (B, L, L, 3)
        dist = torch.norm(diff, dim=-1, keepdim=True)  # (B, L, L, 1)
        direction = diff / (dist + 1e-8)  # unit vectors
        edge_feat = torch.cat([dist, direction], dim=-1)  # (B, L, L, 4)

        # k-nearest neighbors
        _, knn_idx = torch.topk(
            dist.squeeze(-1), self.k, dim=-1, largest={{b1}}
        )

        # Gather neighbor edge features
        knn_idx_exp = knn_idx.unsqueeze(-1).expand(-1, -1, -1, 4)
        knn_edges = torch.gather(edge_feat, 2, knn_idx_exp)

        # Encode edges and aggregate
        encoded = self.edge_encoder(knn_edges)  # (B, L, k, d)
        aggregated = encoded.{{b2}}(dim=2)  # (B, L, d)

        # Node features (init from aggregated neighbors)
        node_feat = torch.cat([aggregated, aggregated], dim=-1)
        node_feat = self.node_update(node_feat)

        logits = self.decoder(node_feat)  # (B, L, 20)
        return logits

model = SimpleInverseFolding()
coords = torch.randn(2, 50, 3) * 10  # fake Ca coords
logits = model(coords)
designed_seq = torch.{{b3}}(logits, dim=-1)
print("Designed sequence shape:", designed_seq.shape)

blanks.hint: Set largest=False in topk to select the k nearest (smallest distance) neighbors.
Use .mean(dim=2) or .sum(dim=2) to aggregate messages from k neighbors.
Use torch.argmax to select the most probable amino acid at each position.

questions.title: UMAP Visualization of Protein Embedding Space
questions.description: Apply UMAP dimensionality reduction to protein language model embeddings and visualize the 2D embedding space colored by protein families, demonstrating how PLMs cluster proteins by function.
questions.info: Visualizing protein LM embedding spaces with UMAP (McInnes et al., arXiv 2018) or t-SNE reveals that models learn biologically meaningful organization. Rives et al. (PNAS 2021) showed that ESM-1b embeddings cluster proteins by structural fold families (SCOP/CATH), even when sequence identity is below 20%. UMAP preserves more global structure than t-SNE, making it preferred for comparing distant protein families. The key hyperparameters are n_neighbors (local vs global structure) and min_dist (cluster tightness). Embeddings from deeper layers tend to show more functionally relevant clustering than shallow layers. This analysis is essential for validating that PLMs capture biological relationships and for exploring the learned protein space. Reference: ESM-1b (Rives et al., PNAS 2021); UMAP (McInnes et al., arXiv 2018).
questions.code:
import numpy as np
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

try:
    from umap import UMAP
except ImportError:
    print("pip install umap-learn")
    # Fallback to t-SNE

def reduce_embeddings(embeddings, method="umap",
                      n_neighbors=15, min_dist=0.1):
    """Reduce PLM embeddings to 2D for visualization.
    embeddings: (N, embed_dim) numpy array
    """
    # Standardize embeddings first
    scaler = StandardScaler()
    scaled = scaler.{{b1}}(embeddings)
    
    if method == "umap":
        reducer = UMAP(
            n_components=2,

            n_components=2,

# Simulate 200 protein embeddings from 4 families
